Hero.description1: I build software products, operate production-grade cloud infrastructure, and design AI systems — from fullstack APIs to bare-metal Kubernetes clusters to RAG pipelines running in production.

Hero.tagline: Architect by mindset, engineer by craft — I unite security, automation, and artificial intelligence.

data.name: Andre
data.title: Fullstack Software Engineer & DevOps Apprentice
data.summary: Software Engineer and Platform Engineering practitioner with a Bac+5 RNCP Level 7 — building production systems that are secure, observable, and AI-native. Author of the minicloud platform: a 67-phase enterprise Kubernetes project running 54+ production workloads, 9/9 OPA Gatekeeper admission policies in deny mode, and a full AI Gateway with RAG, LLMOps, and 62 automated regression checks. Currently an IT Automation Engineer Apprentice at HDI Global SE France, applying the same engineering rigour to real enterprise workflows.

projects.title: Minicloud Platform
projects.purpose: Production-grade enterprise Kubernetes platform built on 4 bare-metal ThinkPad laptops to demonstrate the full private-cloud engineering lifecycle. GitOps-delivered workloads spanning AI/ML, observability, secrets management, SSO/identity, private registry, runtime security, collaboration tools, and platform services — all via ArgoCD app-of-apps. Hardened with zero-trust NetworkPolicy, OPA Gatekeeper admission control in deny mode, and supply-chain security (Cosign, SBOM, GPG-signed commits).
projects.role: Platform Engineer & Solutions Architect. Designed the enterprise GitOps repository structure (app-of-apps pattern, Kustomize Combo 1 base+overlays, 3-branch CI promotion flow, AppProject with locked sourceRepos and clusterResourceWhitelist). Built the zero-trust network topology, the AI Gateway with cloud and local LLM providers (Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Gemini, Ollama) and Presidio PII guardrails, the RAG pipeline (bge-m3 + pgvector HNSW + hybrid BM25 + cross-encoder re-ranking + Docling OCR), and the full regression suite covering the entire platform.
projects.impact: Full automated regression suite continuously green. All OPA Gatekeeper admission policies enforce deny mode with zero violations. Zero-trust NetworkPolicy enforced across all platform namespaces. Vault auto-unseal via AWS KMS — pods restart without operator intervention. Enterprise RAG with sub-400ms embedding latency and sub-1ms HNSW vector scan. AI Gateway routing cloud and local LLM providers with circuit-breaker, per-department key governance, and Langfuse LLMOps tracing.
features: Enterprise GitOps: app-of-apps ArgoCD pattern, Kustomize base+overlays (dev/staging/prod), CI promotes via kustomize edit set image to dev only — PR-gated staging and prod, AppProject with locked sourceRepos and explicit namespace/resource whitelist
All OPA Gatekeeper admission policies in deny mode — zero violations — covering NET_RAW, capabilities, seccomp, privilege escalation, and host namespace access
Zero-trust NetworkPolicy enforced across all platform namespaces — services may only reach declared upstreams
AI Gateway: cloud and local LLM providers (Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Gemini, Ollama), PII/DLP guardrails, cloud fallback chain, Valkey cache
Enterprise RAG: bge-m3 + pgvector HNSW + BM25 hybrid search + cross-encoder re-ranking + Docling OCR
Full supply chain: Cosign keyless signing, syft CycloneDX SBOM, Trivy CVE scan, GPG commits, branch protection, Dependabot
projects.oneLinePitch: A production private-cloud platform on 4 bare-metal ThinkPads — GitOps-delivered, zero-trust hardened, with AI/ML workloads, enterprise RAG, full observability, and supply-chain security from bare metal to running container.
projects.whyItMatters: Most Kubernetes demos run on managed cloud with a credit card. This platform runs on repurposed ThinkPad laptops in a home lab — MAAS bare-metal provisioning, k3s cluster bootstrap, GitOps delivery, secrets management, SSO, observability, runtime security, AI/ML workloads, and collaboration tools all built and operated by one engineer. Every design decision is a real trade-off: Vault auto-unseal via AWS KMS because operator-on-call unseal doesn't scale; Recreate rollout strategy because ResourceQuota doesn't allow a rolling buffer; Restart=always on cloudflared because the tunnel exits clean on a network blip and systemd never notices.
differentiators: App-of-apps GitOps with strict AppProject controls — every cluster resource originates from minicloud-gitops; zero manual kubectl in production; namespaced Applications with locked sourceRepos and clusterResourceWhitelist
Vault auto-unseal via AWS KMS — Vault pods restart without any operator action; static admin passwords absent from every workload's config

OPA Gatekeeper in deny mode with zero violations — not advisory mode; NET_RAW, capabilities, seccomp, and host namespace policies enforced cluster-wide; workloads must comply before reaching Running state
Enterprise RAG pipeline — bge-m3 embeddings + pgvector HNSW index + BM25 hybrid retrieval + cross-encoder re-ranking + Docling OCR for PDF/image ingestion; sub-400ms end-to-end
Full supply chain on every custom image — Trivy CRITICAL exit-code-1, Cosign keyless (GitHub OIDC → Sigstore Fulcio), syft CycloneDX SBOM as OCI referrer, GPG-signed gitops bump commit
architecture: MAAS + OpenTofu → bare metal provisioning; 4 ThinkPad nodes enrolled in MAAS; OpenTofu manages machine lifecycle as infrastructure-as-code
Ansible → k3s bootstrap, CIS kubelet hardening (config.yaml per node), kube-bench k3s-cis-1.7 validation; Tailscale installed on all nodes for VPN mesh

ArgoCD app-of-apps → minicloud-gitops is the single source of truth; apps/ defines namespaced Applications; helm-values/ holds all Helm overrides; services/ holds Kustomize base+overlays for CI/CD services
Vault + ESO → secrets stored in Vault; External Secrets Operator syncs them to Kubernetes Secrets at runtime; AWS KMS auto-unseal; no static credentials in git
Authentik → OIDC provider for all platform services; TOTP enforced; department personas; Cloudflare Tunnel + originServerName fix for TLS SNI on IP-based service URLs
Longhorn → distributed block storage across all nodes; swift-mac (MacBook Pro 2012, Ubuntu 22.04) as primary storage node; Velero daily backup to MinIO on controller
LiteLLM AI Gateway → virtual model routing to Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Gemini, and Ollama (phi4-mini ×3); Presidio PII masking; Valkey cache; Langfuse LLMOps tracing per request
RAG pipeline → rag-ingest service: Docling OCR → bge-m3 embeddings → pgvector HNSW insert; markitdown-proxy for Office/PDF conversion; BM25 hybrid retrieval + cross-encoder re-ranking at query time
Observability → kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus + Grafana + Loki); PrometheusRules for disk/backup alerting; node_exporter on controller (Docker + systemd); ControllerDisk alerts at 85%/90%
Cloudflare Tunnel → public *.devandre.sbs without exposing cluster ingress to the internet; Tailscale for internal access and CI (tag:ci OAuth)

+--------------------------------------------------+

scope: Phase 0–5 — Foundation: MAAS bare-metal provisioning of 4 ThinkPad nodes; k3s cluster bootstrap via Ansible; ArgoCD app-of-apps structure in minicloud-gitops; Helm chart deployment pipeline
Phase 6–15 — Core platform: Vault + ESO secrets management (AWS KMS auto-unseal); Authentik SSO/OIDC with TOTP; Harbor private registry; Longhorn distributed storage; cert-manager + nip.io TLS
Phase 16–25 — Observability + security: kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus + Grafana + Loki); OPA Gatekeeper admission policies in deny mode; Falco runtime security; kube-bench CIS k3s-1.7 hardening

Phase 26–35 — AI/ML layer: LiteLLM AI Gateway with cloud and local LLM providers; Presidio PII/DLP guardrails; RAG pipeline (bge-m3 + pgvector HNSW + BM25 + cross-encoder + Docling OCR); Backstage IDP

Phase 36–45 — Operations: Velero daily backup to MinIO (ClickHouse PV itemOperationTimeout fix); Plane CE project management; minicloud-plane Go service Level 4 integration; Backstage catalog + plugins

Phase 46–70+ — Maturity: VPA auto-scaling; system-upgrade-controller (zero-downtime k3s upgrades); Argo Rollouts canary (platform-demo); Backstage Software Templates golden path; TechDocs; supply-chain hardening across all repos
testPlan: Regression suite: full automated regression checks — OIDC login, catalog entity count, AI Gateway health, RAG ingest pipeline, Gatekeeper violation count, Velero backup status, Cloudflare Tunnel reachability
kube-bench k3s-cis-1.7: 16/16 PASS on all worker nodes; kubelet config.yaml deployed via Ansible CIS hardening playbook
Gatekeeper: all admission policies in deny mode, zero violations; validated by kubectl get constraint -A and checking .status.totalViolations
Velero backup: daily schedule running; ClickHouse PV backed up within 8h itemOperationTimeout; restore tested on non-production namespace
OIDC smoke test: all public *.devandre.sbs services redirect to Authentik login, accept TOTP, and return authenticated session
Supply chain: all custom images have Cosign signature and syft SBOM verifiable via cosign verify and oras referrers; GPG signature on every gitops bump commit
Cloudflare Tunnel: all public URLs reachable without Tailscale; cloudflared systemd service with Restart=always recovers within 5s of network blip
interviewTalkingPoints: Goal: unseal Vault automatically after a node restart — on a bare-metal home lab with no operator on call at 3am. We configured Vault with a single unseal key and sealed it at rest. After the first unplanned power failure, every Vault-dependent workload stayed broken for hours because the pod restarted but the unseal endpoint needed a human. Challenge: operator-on-call unseal doesn't scale and defeats the point of a self-healing cluster. Solution: AWS KMS auto-unseal. The Vault pod calls KMS on startup, the unseal key never touches disk, and the entire cluster recovers from a power failure in under 2 minutes without any human intervention.

Goal: get ArgoCD to stop triggering a rolling restart on Harbor every sync cycle. After adding ignoreDifferences for Harbor's self-signed cert fields, the loop continued. Challenge: Harbor's Helm templates use genSelfSignedCert and randAlphaNum — these generate a new value on every render. With ServerSideApply=true in ArgoCD's syncOptions, ignoreDifferences entries are NOT stripped from the apply payload — ArgoCD sees a diff, self-heals, generates a new RS, triggers a PVC Multi-Attach conflict on RWO volumes, and the loop continues. Solution: remove ServerSideApply=true from the Harbor ArgoCD Application. Client-side 3-way merge correctly strips ignoreDifferences fields, breaking the loop.

Goal: add a pod label (backstage.io/kubernetes-id) to a pre-existing deployment so the Backstage Kubernetes tab could discover it. We committed the label change, ArgoCD synced, and the rollout immediately stalled — new RS pods stuck in FailedCreate with 'admission webhook denied: must not use or retain the NET_RAW capability'. The old pod kept running, the new RS could not create. Challenge: the deployment predates the K8sBlockNetRaw Gatekeeper constraint. The old pod passed admission because it was created before the policy existed. Any new pod (including rollout pods) must comply. Adding the label alone triggered the rollout without adding the required securityContext. Solution: always add capabilities.drop: [NET_RAW] to the container spec in the same commit as any other deployment change on pre-constraint workloads.

Goal: keep the Cloudflare Tunnel running continuously so public *.devandre.sbs URLs stay reachable. The tunnel was configured as a systemd service with Restart=on-failure. After a 1.5-day outage we found cloudflared in the journal as 'exited with code 0' — all 4 tunnel connections had dropped simultaneously on a brief network blip. Systemd interpreted exit code 0 as a clean shutdown and never restarted the process. Challenge: on-failure only restarts when the exit code is non-zero; cloudflared exits cleanly on connection loss. Solution: Restart=always + StartLimitIntervalSec=0. The service now recovers within 5 seconds of any network event regardless of exit code.

Goal: upgrade all 5 k3s nodes from v1.33 to v1.36 with zero downtime and no manual SSH. We used system-upgrade-controller: two Plans (k3s-server and k3s-agent) targeting the new version. The server plan ran first with concurrency 1 and cordon; the agent plan waited for the server plan to complete via a prepare job. Challenge: Velero's upgradeCRDs Helm hook created a Job whose pod completed with exit 0, but k3s v1.36 never populated status.succeeded — the uncountedTerminatedPods field stayed empty, blocking every ArgoCD sync indefinitely. Solution: set upgradeCRDs: false in velero-values.yaml (CRDs were already current) and skipCrds: true in the ArgoCD Application. Re-enable upgradeCRDs on the next actual chart version bump.

projects.title: phi3-financial — Enterprise PromptOps Pipeline
projects.purpose: Domain-restricted AI financial assistant with a production PromptOps architecture. Built a LiteLLM CustomLogger (LangfusePromptHandler) that fetches the active production-labelled prompt from Langfuse at request time with 5-minute in-process cache and a three-layer fail-open chain — enabling instant prompt rollbacks without code deploys or image rebuilds. Includes a 25-case CI eval suite enforcing 100% pass rate as a deployment gate.
projects.role: AI Engineer & Prompt Architect. Designed the 5-section XML prompt architecture (system_intent, domain_knowledge_constraints, safety_and_guardrails, jailbreak_defenses, output_format) with explicit off-topic taxonomy (medical/culinary/gambling/sports, each with named sub-items) and 5 jailbreak defense patterns covering roleplay injection, system override, credential phishing, translation-based bypass, and prompt extraction. Built the three-tier PromptOps infrastructure (Ollama model serving → LiteLLM routing + runtime prompt injection → Langfuse LLMOps), implemented the async_pre_call_hook CustomLogger, and wrote the 25-case behavioral eval suite with semantic versioning and automatic git tagging.
projects.impact: 25/25 eval cases passing (100% — deployment blocked on any regression). Prompt rollback time: under 5 minutes via Langfuse label change — no git push, no pod restart required. Root-caused a 90-second CI outage (ArgoCD Recreate restart overlapping eval runs) and fixed at two layers: RollingUpdate strategy + exponential retry logic in the eval harness.
features: XML-structured Modelfile: 5 behavioral sections (system_intent, domain_constraints, safety_guardrails, jailbreak_defenses, output_format) — explicit prompt architecture, not a single instruction block
5 jailbreak defense patterns: FIXED RUNTIME, UNTRUSTED INPUT, INJECTION RESPONSE, PROMPT ISOLATION, PERSISTENCE — covers roleplay, system override, credential phishing, translation bypass
LangfusePromptHandler: runtime prompt injection via LiteLLM CustomLogger (async_pre_call_hook) — instant rollback, zero redeploy
25-case eval suite across 5 behavioral categories — 100% pass rate enforced as deployment gate, weekly scheduled baseline run
Semantic versioning (MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH) + CI auto git-tagging + fail-open chain: Langfuse → stale cache → env var
projects.oneLinePitch: A production PromptOps pipeline for a domain-restricted financial AI — runtime prompt injection via LiteLLM CustomLogger, instant Langfuse rollback, and a 25-case CI eval gate enforcing 100% pass rate.
projects.whyItMatters: Most LLM deployments treat the system prompt as code — baked into a ConfigMap or image, requiring a full redeploy to change. This pipeline separates prompt governance from infrastructure: the active production prompt lives in Langfuse, gets injected at request time by a LiteLLM CustomLogger, and can be rolled back in under 5 minutes by flipping a label — with no git push and no pod restart. The 25-case CI eval suite enforces behavioral correctness on every commit, treating prompt regressions the same way you'd treat a failing unit test.
differentiators: Runtime prompt injection via LiteLLM CustomLogger (async_pre_call_hook) — prompt fetched from Langfuse at request time, not baked into the image; rollback is a single label flip in the Langfuse UI
Three-layer fail-open chain: live Langfuse → stale in-process cache → ConfigMap env var — LiteLLM never fails because Langfuse is temporarily unreachable
Deterministic 25-case eval suite — no LLM-as-judge, no probabilistic scoring; keyword presence/absence per case; CI blocks on any regression below 100%
Explicit off-topic taxonomy in XML system prompt (medical, culinary, gambling, sports — each with named sub-items), making every domain boundary independently testable
architecture: LangfusePromptHandler (langfuse_prompt_handler.py) — LiteLLM CustomLogger; async_pre_call_hook fetches production-labelled prompt, strips user-supplied system messages (anti-injection), injects authoritative prompt as sole system message; 5-min in-process cache per worker pod

Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant (weight 10) + Ollama phi4-mini ×3 (weight 1 each) — weighted load balancing behind a single LiteLLM virtual model 'phi3-financial'; Groq primary for sub-1s latency, Ollama fallback activates on quota exceeded or API down

Langfuse prompt management — phi3-financial-system versioned with semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH); 'production' label controls which version LangfusePromptHandler fetches at runtime; full audit trail and one-click rollback

sync_prompt_to_litellm.py — fetches production-labelled prompt, renders into a Kubernetes ConfigMap YAML, GPG-signed commit + push to minicloud-gitops; ArgoCD deploys in ~3 min (fast-path fallback if cache TTL matters)
Presidio PII/DLP guardrail — PERSON, PHONE, EMAIL, IP_ADDRESS masked before Langfuse trace logging; DATE_TIME and LOCATION pass through unchanged (financial context entities)
projects.repoBlueprint:
User / Open WebUI
       |
       v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  LiteLLM  (virtual model: phi3-financial)        |
|                                                  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
|  |  LangfusePromptHandler (async_pre_call_hook)|<-----Langfuse Prompt Mgmt
|  |  1. fetch 'production' prompt               | |
|  |     (Valkey 5-min in-process cache)         | |
|  |  2. strip user-supplied system messages     | |
|  |  3. inject as sole system message           | |
|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
|  Presidio PII/DLP  -->  mask PERSON/PHONE/EMAIL  |
|  Circuit Breaker   -->  cooldown 60s / 3 fails   |
+-----------------------+--------------------------+
                        | weighted routing
           +------------+------------+
           |                         |
           v weight: 10              v weight: 1 (x3)
  +------------------+   +--------------------------+
  |  Groq            |   |  Ollama (k3s, ai ns)    |
  |  llama-3.1-8b-   |   |  phi4-mini              |
  |  instant         |   |  primary / secondary /  |
  |  sub-1s latency  |   |  tertiary pods          |
  +------------------+   +--------------------------+
           |                         |
           +------------+------------+
                        | response + trace metadata
                        v
             +----------------------+
             |  Langfuse (LLMOps)  |
             |  trace + score      |
             |  per request        |
             +----------------------+

CI Gate -- prompt-eval.yml (GitHub Actions)
  Tailscale OAuth --> minicloud CA --> LiteLLM /health
  --> eval.py (25 cases, keyword scoring, no LLM judge)
  --> git tag vX.Y.Z on clean main push
  Blocks merge on any result below 25/25 PASS
scope: Phase 1 — XML prompt architecture (Modelfile): designed 5-section XML system prompt with explicit off-topic taxonomy (medical/culinary/gambling/sports with named sub-items) and 5 jailbreak defense patterns; baked into Ollama Modelfile as hardcoded fallback

Phase 2 — LangfusePromptHandler: built LiteLLM CustomLogger (async_pre_call_hook); 5-min in-process cache; three-layer fail-open (live Langfuse → stale cache → ConfigMap env var); strips user-supplied system messages to prevent guardrail bypass

Phase 3 — 25-case eval suite (eval.py): financial knowledge (10 cases), off-topic refusal (4), prompt injection resistance (6), edge/mixed-domain (5); keyword-based scoring — deterministic, no LLM-as-judge; CI exits 1 on any regression
Phase 4 — CI pipeline (prompt-eval.yml): Tailscale OAuth, minicloud CA trust, LiteLLM health check, eval run, auto git-tag on clean main; weekly schedule for baseline drift detection

Phase 5 — Groq routing (minicloud-litellm-custom): groq/llama-3.1-8b-instant as primary (weight 10); Ollama phi4-mini ×3 as fallback (weight 1); eval runtime 30 min → 7 min; chat response 60-90s → sub-1s
testPlan: Eval CI: 25/25 PASS confirmed on Groq backend (run #48, v2.2.4) — T1–T25 all green including T18 (healthcare stocks as investment topic, keyword 'compan' + 'invest')
Jailbreak resistance: T9 (system override), T10 (prompt extraction), T19 (roleplay), T20 (IT admin social engineering), T21 (credential phishing), T22 (translation bypass) — all REFUSE confirmed
Off-topic taxonomy: T6 (recipe), T7 (diabetes), T16 (sports betting odds), T17 (blackjack strategy) — all REFUSE confirmed
PII masking: Presidio masks IBAN, SSN, email, IP_ADDRESS in Langfuse traces; DATE_TIME and LOCATION pass through unchanged (financial context allow-list)
Fail-open chain: Langfuse pod killed mid-eval — stale cache served prompts for 5 min; after TTL, ConfigMap env var fallback activated; LiteLLM never went down
Cloudflare WAF: User-Agent minicloud-eval/1.0 passes; openai SDK default UA (AsyncOpenAI/Python*) gets HTTP 403 — documented in Troubleshooting
interviewTalkingPoints: Goal: run the 25-case eval suite from GitHub Actions without hitting the internal cluster IP. CI runners join the tailnet via Tailscale OAuth, but the tag:ci ACL does not grant access to 10.0.0.200:443. Every eval call returned a TCP timeout. Problem: LITELLM_BASE_URL was set to litellm.10.0.0.200.nip.io — a private IP unreachable from the CI node's network segment even with Tailscale active. Solution: switched LITELLM_BASE_URL to the Cloudflare Tunnel public URL (litellm.devandre.sbs). Requests now route CI → Cloudflare → tunnel → controller → cluster, bypassing the ACL restriction entirely.

Goal: fix a second CI failure after switching to the Cloudflare URL. Every eval call returned HTTP 403 PermissionDeniedError, indistinguishable from an invalid API key at the application layer. Problem: Cloudflare managed WAF blocks the User-Agent set automatically by the openai Python SDK — 'AsyncOpenAI/Python/1.x.x'. The 403 came from Cloudflare before the request reached LiteLLM; the error message looked like an auth failure, not a WAF block. Solution: passed default_headers={'User-Agent': 'minicloud-eval/1.0'} in the OpenAI() constructor. Applies to any Python openai SDK usage against any Cloudflare-fronted endpoint.

Goal: T18 was failing CI despite the model answering correctly. The test asks about pharmaceutical stocks but the model (correctly) handles it as a finance topic — healthcare companies are in-domain. The original required keyword was 'pharmaceut', but the model answered using 'healthcare companies', 'biotech stocks', 'sector analysis' — all correct, none containing 'pharmaceut'. Problem: keyword-based eval is deterministic — a correct answer using synonyms fails the keyword check (false negative). Solution: changed required_keywords to ['compan', 'invest'] — stem fragments present in any correct answer discussing companies or investment concepts. Bumped to PROMPT_VERSION 2.2.4. Lesson: prefer short stem fragments over full domain-specific terms.

Goal: make the eval suite resilient to brief LiteLLM restarts. ArgoCD was using Recreate strategy — any sync causes ~90 seconds of complete downtime. If a sync overlapped a CI eval run, the affected cases failed with ConnectionError even though the model and prompt were correct. Problem: 25 sequential cases × up to 90s of downtime meant a well-timed sync could kill a full run. Solution: implemented three-tier exponential backoff in eval.py — wait 10s / 30s / 60s on 5xx or connection errors before retrying each case. Also switched LiteLLM to RollingUpdate strategy to prevent future Recreate downtime.

liveEndpoints.label: LiteLLM API — phi3-financial virtual model

projects.title: Enterprise RAG Pipeline
projects.purpose: Built a full multilingual RAG pipeline for French insurance document retrieval across three custom-built repos. minicloud-markitdown-proxy (FastAPI): routes documents by type — PDF/images to Docling OCR, Office/HTML to MarkItDown in-pod — exposing a Docling-compatible API surface. minicloud-rag-ingest (FastAPI): full ingestion pipeline in one HTTP endpoint — convert → structure-aware heading chunk (French insurance regex) → bge-m3 1024-dim embed → pgvector HNSW INSERT. minicloud-postgresql-noavx512: custom PostgreSQL image with pgvector's vector.so rebuilt without -mavx512f/bw/vl/dq to fix a SIGILL crash on i7-8565U/i7-10510U CPUs caused by AVX-512 EVEX instructions in pgvector 0.8.4. Hybrid search layer: BM25 with French Snowball stemmer + NLTK stop-words injected via init container patching rank_bm25/utils.py at pod start. Cross-encoder re-ranking (ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2) filters to top-3.
projects.role: Systems Engineer & ML Engineer. Diagnosed the SIGILL root cause (pgvector 0.8.4 HNSW code emitting AVX-512 EVEX instructions not supported by i7-8565U/i7-10510U), rebuilt vector.so from source with AVX-512 disabled, and packaged it as a custom Harbor image. Designed the two-microservice ingestion architecture, wrote the French BM25 preprocessor (NLTK Snowball stemmer + hardcoded insurance stop-word set patched into rank_bm25/utils.py via init container subPath mount), and wired Docling as the PDF/OCR backend. Both microservices shipped with Dockerfile, GitHub Actions CI, and cosign keyless signing.
projects.impact: Unblocked all HNSW index operations on CPUs without AVX-512 — zero SIGILL crashes post-fix. Measured pipeline latencies: HNSW vector scan 0.31ms, BM25 GIN FTS 2.71ms, INSERT + HNSW update 6.8ms/row, bge-m3 embedding 393ms. French stemming verified: sinistres/sinistre → sinistr ✅, réassurance/réassurer → réassur ✅. Full end-to-end in-cluster: PDF upload → Docling OCR → heading-aware chunks → 1024-dim vectors → pgvector HNSW + BM25 hybrid → cross-encoder top-3. No external API calls.
features: minicloud-markitdown-proxy: PDF/images → Docling OCR, Office/HTML → MarkItDown in-pod (Docling-compatible API)
minicloud-rag-ingest: single HTTP endpoint — convert → chunk → bge-m3 embed → pgvector HNSW INSERT
minicloud-postgresql-noavx512: vector.so rebuilt without AVX-512 — fixed SIGILL on i7-8565U/i7-10510U (pgvector 0.8.4 SIMD bug)
French BM25: NLTK Snowball stemmer + insurance stop-words patched into rank_bm25 via Kubernetes init container
Hybrid retrieval: bge-m3 1024-dim vector + BM25 + cross-encoder re-ranking (ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2, top-3)
Supply chain: Dockerfile + GitHub Actions CI + cosign keyless signing on both microservices
Generation step routed through Enterprise AI Gateway (LiteLLM) — RAG retrieval + cloud/local model selection in one pipeline
stack: PostgreSQL + pgvector (custom noavx512 build, C / Makefile)
projects.oneLinePitch: A three-repo multilingual RAG pipeline — French insurance document ingestion from PDF upload to pgvector HNSW in one HTTP call, with a custom PostgreSQL image to fix an AVX-512 SIGILL crash on cluster CPUs.
projects.whyItMatters: Off-the-shelf RAG tooling splits documents by character count and has no awareness of legal structure. French insurance contracts are organised around articles, clauses, and annexes — a chunk that straddles two articles loses the legal context of both. This pipeline splits at heading boundaries, tags every chunk with doc_type/section/article metadata, and stores it in a vector+BM25 hybrid index so retrieval is both semantically accurate and keyword-precise. The whole pipeline runs in-cluster with no external API calls during ingestion — a hard requirement for handling confidential insurance documents.
differentiators: Structure-aware chunking — splits on French insurance heading patterns (Article, Chapitre, Titre, Annexe, Garantie) before falling back to paragraph boundaries; character-count splitters would cut across article boundaries

Custom PostgreSQL image (minicloud-postgresql-noavx512) — diagnosed a SIGILL crash caused by pgvector 0.8.4 emitting AVX-512 EVEX instructions; rebuilt vector.so from source with -mno-avx512f/bw/vl/dq; unblocked HNSW on all 4 cluster CPUs (i7-8565U / i7-10510U)

French BM25 via init container patching — rank_bm25 uses whitespace tokenisation by default; NLTK Snowball French stemmer + insurance stop-word set injected by patching rank_bm25/utils.py at pod start via a ConfigMap subPath mount — zero image rebuilds required

Docling-compatible proxy (minicloud-markitdown-proxy) — routes scanned images to the heavy Docling OCR pod and handles text-based PDFs/Office files with MarkItDown in-pod; both consumers (Open WebUI + rag-ingest) call the same endpoint without knowing which backend ran
architecture: minicloud-markitdown-proxy (github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-markitdown-proxy) — FastAPI proxy; scanned images go to Docling OCR pod (ghcr.io/docling-project/docling-serve-cpu:v1.26.0, 4.4 GB, Helm-deployed); PDFs + Office files go to MarkItDown (PyMuPDF, in-pod); response format mimics Docling /v1/convert/file so Open WebUI DOCLING_SERVER_URL points here unchanged

minicloud-rag-ingest (github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-rag-ingest) — FastAPI ingestion service; POST /ingest: forward to markitdown-proxy → structure-aware heading chunker (French insurance regex) → batch embed via LiteLLM text-embedding-3-small (1536-dim, batch 64) → pgvector INSERT with vmetadata JSONB; fully in-cluster, no local tools required

minicloud-postgresql-noavx512 (github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-postgresql-noavx512) — custom Bitnami PostgreSQL 18.4.0 image; vector.so rebuilt from source with -mno-avx512f/bw/vl/dq to fix SIGILL on i7-8565U/i7-10510U; HNSW index on vector(1536) + GIN index with french dictionary

Hybrid retrieval — HNSW vector scan (0.31ms) + BM25 GIN FTS (2.71ms); BM25 uses rank_bm25 patched with NLTK Snowball French stemmer via init container; cross-encoder ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 re-ranks and filters to top-3
GitOps delivery — all three services deployed via ArgoCD; each has GitHub Actions CI: docker build → Harbor push via Tailscale → cosign keyless sign → GPG-signed gitops bump into minicloud-gitops
projects.repoBlueprint:
minicloud-markitdown-proxy/   github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-markitdown-proxy
  main.py                      FastAPI proxy — routing by file extension
  Dockerfile                   python:3.12-slim, UID 1000, port 8000
  .github/workflows/ci.yml     build -> Harbor -> cosign -> gitops bump

minicloud-rag-ingest/        github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-rag-ingest
  main.py                      FastAPI service — convert -> chunk -> embed -> INSERT
  Dockerfile                   python:3.12-slim, UID 1000, port 8001
  .github/workflows/ci.yml     build -> Harbor -> cosign -> gitops bump

minicloud-postgresql-noavx512/  github.com/andrelair-platform/minicloud-postgresql-noavx512
  Dockerfile                   Bitnami 18.4.0 base + vector.so rebuilt -mno-avx512f
  .github/workflows/ci.yml     build -> Harbor -> cosign
scope: Phase 1 — Conversion layer (minicloud-markitdown-proxy): needed a single endpoint that both Open WebUI and rag-ingest could call without knowing which backend ran; built a Docling-compatible proxy routing by file extension

Phase 2 — SIGILL diagnosis and fix (minicloud-postgresql-noavx512): every HNSW INSERT crashed with SIGILL on all 4 cluster CPUs; traced to pgvector 0.8.4 emitting AVX-512 EVEX instructions; rebuilt vector.so without AVX-512 and packaged as a custom Harbor image

Phase 3 — Ingestion service (minicloud-rag-ingest): replaced the Mac-side Python script with an in-cluster FastAPI service; structure-aware chunker replaces character-count splitting; batch embedding via LiteLLM

Phase 4 — French BM25: rank_bm25 default tokeniser is whitespace-only — sinistres != sinistre; injected NLTK Snowball stemmer + insurance stop-words by patching rank_bm25/utils.py at pod start via init container + ConfigMap subPath
Phase 5 — Cross-encoder re-ranking: ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 runs in-process in the Open WebUI pod; re-ranks BM25+HNSW candidates and filters to top-3 before passing to the LLM
Phase 6 — Supply chain hardening: all three repos have GitHub Actions CI with Trivy scan, cosign keyless signing, and GPG-signed gitops bump; images stored in Harbor with cosign signatures
testPlan: SIGILL regression: kubectl exec into postgresql-ai pod, CREATE INDEX USING hnsw, INSERT a vector row — confirmed no SIGILL across all 4 cluster nodes (i7-8565U x2, i7-10510U x2)
French BM25 stemming: exec into open-webui pod, call _french_bm25_preprocess('sinistres') — expected output 'sinistr'; also verified: sinistre->sinistr, reassurance->reassur
End-to-end ingestion: port-forward rag-ingest, POST a French insurance PDF — response shows chunks_stored > 0 and metadata_sample.article populated; verified row count in ragdb

Hybrid retrieval quality: Open WebUI query for BNP Paribas CET1 ratio — verified retrieved chunks contained numeric CET1 values (HNSW semantic match) and BNP Paribas keyword (BM25 match) in top results
markitdown-proxy routing: POST a scanned TIFF — logs show route to Docling; POST a .docx — logs show MarkItDown path; both return the Docling-compatible response format
Pipeline latency baseline: HNSW scan 0.31ms, GIN FTS 2.71ms, INSERT + HNSW update 6.8ms/row — all within SLA targets
interviewTalkingPoints: Goal: run HNSW vector indexing on a self-hosted Kubernetes cluster with consumer-grade Intel CPUs. Every HNSW INSERT was crashing the PostgreSQL pod with SIGILL — a hardware-level illegal instruction fault. Problem: pgvector 0.8.4 introduced SIMD-accelerated HNSW code that emits AVX-512 EVEX-encoded instructions. All 4 cluster CPUs (Intel i7-8565U and i7-10510U, Whiskey Lake) support AVX2 but not AVX-512. The fault appeared only on HNSW operations, not IVFFlat, which initially obscured the root cause. Solution: rebuilt vector.so from source with -mno-avx512f/bw/vl/dq, packaged as a custom Harbor image, validated with direct INSERT tests on all 4 nodes. Zero SIGILL crashes since deployment.

Goal: make BM25 keyword retrieval work for French legal text. Open WebUI hybrid search runs rank_bm25 alongside HNSW. rank_bm25 tokenises by whitespace only — sinistres, sinistre, and sinistres are three different tokens with zero overlap. For French insurance documents where queries and clauses use different inflections of the same term, this makes BM25 almost useless. Problem: we could not modify rank_bm25 (it runs inside the Open WebUI pod, not our image). Solution: init container that patches rank_bm25/utils.py at pod start — injects an NLTK Snowball French stemmer and a hardcoded insurance stop-word set — via a ConfigMap subPath volume that shadows the original file. The patch applies every restart with no image rebuild.

Goal: eliminate the Mac-side ingestion dependency. The original pipeline required a local Python script, local DB port-forward, and local credentials to ingest documents — creating a hard dependency on one developer's machine. Solution: minicloud-rag-ingest is a cluster-native FastAPI service accepting a single multipart POST. The full pipeline runs in-cluster: convert via markitdown-proxy, structure-aware chunk, embed via LiteLLM, INSERT into ragdb. Any pod in the ai namespace — or any developer with a port-forward — can ingest a document with one curl call.

Goal: build a conversion layer for both scanned documents (needing OCR) and machine-generated PDFs and Office files. Running everything through Docling would work but its CPU image is 4.4 GB and its layout analysis uses hundreds of MB per page — impractical for a CPU-only cluster handling mostly text-based files. Solution: minicloud-markitdown-proxy routes by file extension: scanned images go to Docling, everything else is handled in-pod with MarkItDown (PyMuPDF for PDFs). The API contract matches Docling /v1/convert/file, so both Open WebUI and rag-ingest call the same endpoint without knowing which backend ran.

projects.title: Enterprise AI Gateway
projects.purpose: Multi-provider LLM routing and enterprise governance platform built on LiteLLM on Kubernetes. Unified 8 cloud and local providers (Groq, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek, Mistral, Anthropic Claude, HuggingFace featherless-ai via router.huggingface.co, and local Ollama) behind a single API endpoint with intelligent fallback routing (Ollama→Groq→DeepSeek), Presidio PII/DLP scrubbing, 3-tier department key governance with per-dept budget caps and model allowlists, circuit breaker protection, Valkey prompt cache, and full Langfuse LLMOps tracing. Cost visibility delivered via a Grafana dashboard reading LiteLLM's PostgreSQL usage tables. Includes Langfuse v3.201.1 with ClickHouse analytics backend and Authentik OIDC SSO, plus an NVIDIA NIM tier (nemotron-70b, llama-8b, deepseek-r1).
projects.role: AI Platform Engineer. Designed the 8-provider routing topology and 3-stage fallback chain, implemented Presidio PII/DLP as a pre-call LiteLLM guardrail with a financial-context entity allow-list (DATE_TIME and LOCATION excluded to avoid masking years and country references), built the 3-tier department key governance model with per-dept budget caps and model allowlists, configured the circuit breaker (cooldown_time=60s, allowed_fails=3), deployed Langfuse v3.201.1 with ClickHouse+Valkey and wired Authentik OIDC for SSO access, and built the Grafana cost dashboard with SQL queries against LiteLLM's PostgreSQL usage tables.
projects.impact: 8 LLM providers unified behind one endpoint — cloud or local, transparently. deepseek-r1:7b cloud-first cuts inference from 40+s (local CPU) to 5-8s (DeepSeek cloud API). Automatic failover: applications survive a provider outage with zero code changes. Department budget caps enforce FinOps boundaries at the API layer. Every request traced in Langfuse with cost, latency, and model metadata for full audit.
features: 8-provider routing: Groq, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek, Mistral, Anthropic Claude, HuggingFace featherless-ai, Ollama — single LiteLLM endpoint
Intelligent fallback chain: Ollama → Groq → DeepSeek — automatic failover on provider unavailability
Presidio PII/DLP guardrail — pre-call PII masking with financial-context entity allow-list (years, locations, URLs excluded)
3-tier department key governance: per-dept budget caps, model allowlists, and usage quotas
Circuit breaker (cooldown=60s, allowed_fails=3) + Valkey prompt cache (exact-match, 600s TTL) — resilience and cost optimisation
Langfuse LLMOps (v3.201.1 + ClickHouse + Valkey + Authentik OIDC) + Grafana cost dashboard via PostgreSQL SQL
architecture: Model aliasing layer: friendly names (e.g. 'gpt-4o', 'claude-sonnet') map to provider-specific model IDs in LiteLLM config — callers use stable aliases regardless of which provider is actually serving the request

Budget enforcement at the API key layer, not the application: LiteLLM VirtualKey stores max_budget + allowed_models per department; the gateway rejects overspent or out-of-allowlist requests before they reach any provider

Presidio pre_call_hook: DATE_TIME and LOCATION entities explicitly excluded from the Presidio analyzer — a necessity for financial queries where '2024' and 'France' are meaningful data, not PII to scrub

ClickHouse as Langfuse analytics backend instead of PostgreSQL: high-cardinality trace writes (cost, latency, tokens per request) saturate PostgreSQL write throughput at eval-pipeline scale; ClickHouse OLAP handles the same workload with no schema contention

Grafana dashboard provisioned declaratively: cost dashboard JSON stored in git as a ConfigMap labelled grafana_dashboard: '1' — Grafana sidecar watches for the label and injects it without any UI interaction; PostgreSQL datasource password injected via envValueFrom from an ESO-managed secret
scope: Phase 1 — Core gateway: LiteLLM with local Ollama; department virtual keys with model allowlists and per-key budget caps
Phase 2 — Cloud expansion: Groq, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek, Mistral, Anthropic Claude, HuggingFace featherless-ai added; 3-stage fallback chain Ollama→Groq→DeepSeek configured
Phase 3 — PII/DLP: Presidio integrated as LiteLLM pre_call_hook; financial-context allow-list tuned to stop DATE_TIME masking years and LOCATION masking country names in financial queries
Phase 4 — Resilience: Circuit breaker (cooldown=60s, allowed_fails=3) + Valkey exact-match prompt cache (600s TTL) added; deepseek-r1:7b moved to cloud-first routing (5–8s vs 40+s on local CPU)

Phase 5 — LLMOps: Langfuse v3.201.1 deployed with ClickHouse analytics backend + Valkey trace cache; Authentik OIDC SSO wired; every LiteLLM request traced with cost, latency, model, and department tag
Phase 6 — Cost visibility: Grafana dashboard built with 6 SQL panels against LiteLLM_SpendLogs; PostgreSQL datasource provisioned via ESO + Grafana sidecar ConfigMap injection
Phase 7 — NVIDIA NIM tier: nemotron-70b, llama-8b, deepseek-r1 added as a premium model tier; API key synced from Vault via ESO ExternalSecret
testPlan: Department key enforcement: 16 virtual keys (dept-it, dept-cyber, dept-data…) — each verified to reject models outside its allowlist and block calls once budget is exhausted
Fallback chain smoke test: Ollama pod killed mid-request → confirmed automatic failover to Groq within circuit breaker cooldown window; zero application-side changes required

PII guardrail verification: test prompts with IBAN, SSN, email addresses — masking confirmed; years (2024, 2025) and country names (France, Belgium) verified to pass through the financial allow-list unchanged
Grafana dashboard query test: SQL executed from Grafana Explore against LiteLLM_SpendLogs — row count matched expected request volume; rawQuery:true confirmed required for Grafana PostgreSQL plugin
Langfuse trace audit: spot-check 10 LiteLLM requests — each verified as a Langfuse trace with correct cost attribution, model name, and department tag attached

Regression integration: LiteLLM /health, Langfuse tracing active, Grafana datasource reachable, and dept key routing all included in the 62-check automated regression suite (regression checks #17–#26 — 62 PASS / 0 FAIL)

projects.title: Enterprise AI Chat Platform
projects.purpose: Deployed Open WebUI as a production enterprise AI chat interface serving 16 business departments through Authentik OIDC SSO. Connects the full AI backend stack — LiteLLM AI Gateway (8 providers), RAG pipeline (French insurance knowledge base), and SearXNG real-time web search — into a single product accessible to non-technical business users. phi3-financial serves as the default model for finance analysts, domain-restricted and prompt-governed at runtime via Langfuse. Conversation history and user management backed by PostgreSQL for production-grade persistence.
projects.role: Platform & AI Product Engineer. Wired Authentik OIDC into Open WebUI, resolving SSL verification failure against the self-signed cluster CA via an init container that concatenates system CAs and the minicloud CA into a single bundle (SSL_CERT_FILE=/ca-bundle/bundle.crt). Deployed SearXNG with two non-obvious gotchas: enableServiceLinks=false to prevent Kubernetes injecting a SEARXNG_PORT env var that crashes granian, and BYPASS_WEB_SEARCH_WEB_LOADER=true for news site scraping compatibility. Migrated conversation storage from SQLite to PostgreSQL and wired the AI Gateway and RAG pipeline as backends.
projects.impact: 16 business departments with SSO-controlled AI access — each user sees only their own conversation history. Finance analysts get phi3-financial by default: domain-restricted, prompt-rolled-back in under 5 minutes via Langfuse. Users query a French insurance document knowledge base directly in chat via the RAG pipeline. Real-time web search via self-hosted SearXNG. Zero conversation data loss on pod restarts — PostgreSQL-backed.
features: Authentik OIDC SSO — 16 department groups, per-dept model defaults, conversation isolation enforced by design
phi3-financial default for finance — domain-restricted, runtime prompt injection via Langfuse (instant rollback, no redeploy)
Enterprise RAG in chat — French insurance knowledge base queryable via bge-m3 hybrid search + cross-encoder re-ranking
SearXNG real-time web search — self-hosted meta-search, no tracking, globe icon in chat UI
PostgreSQL conversation persistence — production-grade, migrated from SQLite, zero data loss on pod restart
SSL CA bundle init container — solved OIDC token/JWKS SSL verification failure against self-signed cluster CA

projects.title: Backstage Internal Developer Portal
projects.purpose: Production-hardened Internal Developer Portal built on Backstage 1.52, deployed on a self-hosted Kubernetes cluster with GitOps delivery, Authentik OIDC SSO, Software Templates, TechDocs, and a custom Plane Issues plugin.
projects.role: Platform Engineer — designed and implemented the custom frontend system, OIDC integration, Tech Radar module, Software Templates scaffolder, Plane Issues plugin, TechDocs integration, CI/CD pipeline, and GitOps delivery via ArgoCD.
projects.impact: Single pane of glass for all cluster services — AI/ML workloads (RAG pipeline, AI gateway, phi3-financial, Open WebUI), platform tools (IDP, project tracker, password manager), CI/CD services, infrastructure (Nextcloud, OnlyOffice), and APIs. Full Swagger UI per API. Live Kubernetes pod status per component. Tech Radar with 39 entries. Golden-path Software Template generates repo + CI + GitOps manifest + ArgoCD app in one click. In-portal TechDocs per service. Live Plane Issues tab per catalog entity. Fully automated GitOps — zero manual steps after git push.
features: Software Catalog — all cluster workloads tracked: AI/ML services (RAG pipeline, AI gateway, Open WebUI, phi3-financial), platform tools (IDP, project tracker, password manager), CI/CD services (platform-demo, ktayl-solution-web), infrastructure (Nextcloud, OnlyOffice), and APIs — with owner, system, lifecycle, and tag metadata
API Docs — inline Swagger/OpenAPI UI rendered for every registered API entity (e.g. rag-ingest-api: GET /health, POST /ingest)
Kubernetes live view — real-time pod count and error status per component pulled from the live k3s cluster
Custom Tech Radar — 4-quadrant radar (Platforms, AI & ML, Security, Languages) with 39 technology entries, fetched live from minicloud-gitops with no rebuild required
Software Templates — golden-path go-service scaffolder; generates GitHub repo, CI pipeline, GitOps manifests, and ArgoCD app in one click
TechDocs — in-portal documentation rendered from mkdocs.yml per service; mkdocs-techdocs-core baked into the Backstage image
Plane Issues tab — custom @internal/plugin-minicloud-plane plugin showing live Plane CE project issues per catalog entity
Authentik OIDC SSO — single sign-on via Authentik; no separate Backstage user accounts; TOTP enforced
GitOps CI/CD — Trivy CVE scan + Cosign keyless signing + syft CycloneDX SBOM on every image; GPG-signed gitops bump; ArgoCD Recreate rollout
projects.oneLinePitch: A production IDP on Kubernetes — single pane of glass for all cluster services, live API docs, Kubernetes status, Software Templates, TechDocs, Plane Issues, and a custom Tech Radar, all delivered via GitOps.
projects.whyItMatters: As platform complexity grows, engineers spend more time discovering where things live than building. Backstage centralises service ownership, API contracts, live cluster state, task tracking, and documentation into one authenticated portal — making the platform self-documenting and giving every new service a golden path from day one.
differentiators: Custom Tech Radar module — the npm plugin silently dropped at runtime due to a nested frontend-plugin-api version conflict; rebuilt as a workspace-local createFrontendPlugin; content fetched live from minicloud-gitops with zero image rebuilds
Plane Issues custom plugin — @internal/plugin-minicloud-plane; connects catalog entities to live Plane CE project issues via a proxy endpoint; zero external SaaS dependency
Software Templates golden path — scaffolder generates GitHub repo + CI skeleton + GitOps manifests + ArgoCD Application in one click; new service goes from zero to deployed in under 10 minutes
TechDocs integrated — mkdocs-techdocs-core baked into the Backstage image; documentation rendered in-portal from mkdocs.yml per service; no separate docs site
Supply chain hardened — every image is Trivy-scanned, Cosign-signed (keyless via GitHub OIDC → Sigstore Fulcio), and syft CycloneDX SBOM attached as OCI referrer
architecture: Frontend: createApp() from @backstage/frontend-defaults — plugins registered as typed FrontendFeature objects; custom plugins as workspace-local createFrontendPlugin
Auth: SignInPageBlueprint.make() with Authentik OIDC; metadataUrl aligned to auth.devandre.sbs (not nip.io) to match cookie domain and prevent silent session expiry loop
Tech Radar: custom createFrontendPlugin at /tech-radar; fetches tech-radar.json from minicloud-gitops live; zero image rebuild to update content

Plane Issues: @internal/plugin-minicloud-plane workspace plugin; EntityPlaneIssuesContent tab; proxy /minicloud-plane → minicloud-plane.minicloud-plane-dev.svc.cluster.local:8080 in backstage-values.yaml
TechDocs: mkdocs-techdocs-core installed in Backstage image; local builder config in backstage-values.yaml; mkdocs.yml + docs/ per service repo
Software Templates: go-service template in minicloud-backstage/templates/; steps: fetch:template → publish:github → branch:create (dev, staging) → PR to minicloud-gitops → catalog:register
Backend: Node 24 (trixie-slim); PostgreSQL via Bitnami subchart on Longhorn PVC
CI: yarn build:backend → Docker → Harbor (Tailscale) → Trivy → cosign sign → syft SBOM → GPG gitops bump
GitOps: ArgoCD Recreate strategy (avoids rolling-update quota deadlock in single-replica + ResourceQuota setup)
projects.repoBlueprint:
GitHub Actions CI
  yarn build → Docker → Harbor
  → Trivy scan → cosign sign
  → syft SBOM (OCI referrer)
  → GPG gitops bump commit
         |
         v (ArgoCD webhook)

  Plane CE (plane.devandre.sbs)
scope: Phase 1 — Bootstrap: bare Backstage 1.52 deployed via ArgoCD Helm chart; PostgreSQL subchart on Longhorn PVC; app-config injected via Helm ConfigMap
Phase 2 — OIDC SSO: Authentik provider configured (grant_types, HS256 signing key, property_mappings); SignInPageBlueprint wired; metadataUrl aligned to devandre.sbs to fix silent session expiry
Phase 3 — Catalog population: 14 → 19 Components, 1 System, 10 Locations; catalog rules updated; root route mapped explicitly to catalog
Phase 4 — Kubernetes plugin: ServiceAccount token + CA cert injected via ESO-managed secret; live pod status per component
Phase 5 — API Docs: OpenAPI entities registered; full Swagger UI rendered inline per API entity

Phase 6 — Custom Tech Radar: @backstage/plugin-tech-radar dropped (nested frontend-plugin-api@0.6.7 vs workspace 0.17.2 causes silent drop); rebuilt as workspace-local createFrontendPlugin; 39 entries across 4 quadrants
Phase 7 — Supply chain hardening: Trivy CRITICAL scan; keyless Cosign; syft CycloneDX SBOM; GPG gitops bump; Recreate strategy
Phase 8 — Plane Issues plugin: @internal/plugin-minicloud-plane workspace plugin; EntityPlaneIssuesContent tab; proxy configured in backstage-values.yaml
Phase 9 — TechDocs: mkdocs-techdocs-core installed in Backstage image; local builder configured in backstage-values.yaml; mkdocs.yml + docs/ per service
Phase 10 — Software Templates: go-service golden-path scaffolder; backstage-github-secret wired from Vault ci-secrets; template generates repo + CI + GitOps + ArgoCD app in one click
testPlan: OIDC login smoke test: navigate to backstage.devandre.sbs → Sign In → Authentik → redirects to catalog as authenticated user (tested across 3 Authentik department personas)
Catalog entity count: entity count visible in Software Catalog header; validated against bn_backstage DB count in the backstage pod
API Docs render test: catalog → rag-ingest-api → DEFINITION tab → Swagger UI loads GET /health, GET /ready, POST /ingest with request/response schemas
Kubernetes live tab: catalog → rag-ingest → KUBERNETES tab → shows 1 pod Running, 0 errors against live cluster
Tech Radar render test: /tech-radar → 4 quadrants render; modified tech-radar.json in minicloud-gitops → radar updated without image rebuild
TechDocs: catalog → minicloud-backstage → DOCS tab → mkdocs documentation renders in-portal
Software Templates: /create → go-service → fill form → scaffolder creates GitHub repo + dev/staging branches + GitOps PR → catalog registers entity
Plane Issues tab: catalog → platform-demo → PLANE ISSUES tab → shows live issues from Plane CE project PT
CI gate: push to main → GitHub Actions build + Trivy scan → Harbor → cosign sign → syft SBOM → ArgoCD Recreate → backstage pod 1/1 Ready
interviewTalkingPoints: Goal: give platform engineers a self-hosted Tech Radar without relying on an external SaaS. We installed @backstage/plugin-tech-radar from npm, wired it into createApp(), deployed, and saw nothing. No error, no crash — a blank sidebar slot. Challenge: @backstage/plugin-tech-radar ships a nested frontend-plugin-api@0.6.7 while the workspace uses 0.17.2. The $type symbol check in the plugin registry fails silently — plugin loads without error but is never registered. Solution: stripped the npm package, rebuilt Tech Radar as a workspace-local createFrontendPlugin using the workspace version. The radar now fetches tech-radar.json live from minicloud-gitops — updating content requires zero image rebuilds.

Goal: surface live Plane CE project issues in Backstage per catalog entity — single pane of glass across code, infrastructure, and task tracking. We wrote @internal/plugin-minicloud-plane as a workspace-local Backstage plugin, rendering an EntityPlaneIssuesContent tab. Challenge: minicloud-plane repo was private — Backstage catalog discovery fetches raw.githubusercontent.com URLs, which return 404 for private repos. Solution: made the repo public. This also unblocked the catalog registration URL used in the scaffolder template.

Goal: stand up Software Templates so new services have a golden path from zero to deployed without manual steps. The scaffolder generates a GitHub repo with CI skeleton, GitOps manifests, and an ArgoCD Application via a 6-step template. Challenge: Nunjucks template syntax conflicts with GitHub Actions ${{ }} expression syntax. Solution: wrap CI skeleton content in {% raw %}...{% endraw %} blocks; use github.event.repository.name instead of the GHA expression inside the skeleton.

Goal: deploy Backstage reliably with ArgoCD in a namespace with a tight ResourceQuota (2 CPU / 2 Gi, single replica). On the first few deploys everything worked, but adding config changes caused new pods to get stuck Pending. Challenge: RollingUpdate briefly runs two pods simultaneously — second pod always blocked until first is evicted, which never happens (Kubernetes waits for new pod to be Ready first). Circular dependency with no timeout. Solution: strategy.type: Recreate. Peak usage stays at 1× the quota at all times.

Goal: secure Backstage behind Authentik OIDC. Login worked but after ~10 minutes of idle the session silently expired and the page went blank with a redirect loop. Challenge: Authentik binds the session cookie to devandre.sbs. The backend metadataUrl was set to auth.10.0.0.200.nip.io — a different origin from the cookie domain. The browser's prompt=none silent refresh is rejected by Authentik. The error only surfaces after the initial token TTL expires. Solution: aligned metadataUrl to auth.devandre.sbs to match the cookie domain exactly.

screenshots.title: Software Catalog — All Cluster Services
screenshots.caption: All cluster workloads in one view — AI/ML services, platform tools, CI/CD services, infrastructure, and APIs — with tags, lifecycle, owner, and system metadata. backstage.io/kubernetes-id annotation wires each entity to the live K8s tab.

screenshots.title: Tech Radar — 39 Entries
screenshots.caption: Custom 4-quadrant radar: Platforms, AI & ML, Security & Identity, Languages & Frameworks. 39 technology entries with ADOPT / TRIAL / ASSESS / HOLD rings. Data fetched live from minicloud-gitops — no rebuild needed.

screenshots.title: API Docs — Swagger UI
screenshots.caption: Inline Swagger UI for the rag-ingest service OpenAPI spec: GET /health, GET /ready, POST /ingest. Rendered directly from the API entity registered in the catalog.

screenshots.title: Kubernetes Live View
screenshots.caption: Live Kubernetes tab — 1 pod running, no errors, sourced from the minicloud k3s cluster in real time via the backstage.io/kubernetes-id annotation.

screenshots.title: Software Templates — Golden Path
screenshots.caption: go-service scaffolder: fill in service name and owner → Backstage creates GitHub repo, CI pipeline skeleton, GitOps manifests, and ArgoCD Application automatically.

screenshots.title: TechDocs — In-Portal Docs
screenshots.caption: mkdocs-techdocs-core renders service documentation in-portal. No external doc site — docs live alongside the code, rendered from mkdocs.yml in each service repo.
